question: Question 1. Tell me about yourself
answer:
I'm a senior React developer with several years of experience building production applications — dashboards, admin tools, and consumer-facing products used by real traffic.

I spend a lot of time not just writing components, but thinking about how state flows through an app, why re-renders happen, and how to keep a large codebase maintainable as it grows past a handful of contributors.

I've worked across the whole lifecycle — from setting up a new app's architecture, to optimizing a slow one, to mentoring other engineers on patterns like compound components and custom hooks.

What I enjoy most about React is that it rewards understanding the mental model — once you really get how rendering, hooks, and reconciliation work together, most bugs and performance issues stop being mysterious.

question: Question 2. What does JSX actually compile down to, and what is React.createElement doing under the hood?
answer:
JSX isn't something the browser understands — it's syntactic sugar that a compiler like Babel or the TypeScript compiler transforms into plain JavaScript function calls before it ever runs.

Historically, every JSX tag compiled into a call to React.createElement, passing the tag name or component, an object of props, and any children. So a div with a className and some text became a function call that returns a plain JavaScript object describing that element — not a real DOM node.

Modern tooling actually uses a newer JSX transform, which compiles to calls like jsx and jsxs from the react/jsx-runtime module instead of React.createElement directly. It's a more optimized version of the same idea, and it's why you no longer need to import React just to use JSX.

Either way, the result is the same conceptually — JSX becomes a tree of lightweight objects, called React elements, that describe what the UI should look like. React then reads that tree to figure out what to actually render.
bonus: You can see this transformation yourself on the Babel REPL — pasting in JSX and watching it turn into function calls is a great way to build intuition for what React is actually doing.

question: Question 3. How does React's reconciliation algorithm actually decide what to update in the DOM?
answer:
The Virtual DOM is really just the tree of React elements I mentioned — plain objects describing the UI. Reconciliation is the algorithm React uses to compare a new tree against the previous one and figure out the minimal set of real DOM changes needed.

A truly generic tree-diffing algorithm would be extremely slow — comparing two arbitrary trees is expensive. React avoids that by using heuristics based on how real UIs actually change. If two elements at the same position have different types, React doesn't try to find similarities — it tears down the old subtree and builds a new one from scratch. If they're the same type, React keeps the existing DOM node and just updates its changed attributes.

For lists of children, React relies on keys to match up elements between renders instead of comparing them by position. Without keys, React falls back to comparing children by index, which is where a lot of the classic list bugs come from.

Because of these heuristics, reconciliation runs in linear time relative to the number of elements, instead of the cubic time a naive tree-diff would take.
bonus: This heuristic-based approach is a deliberate trade-off — it's not the most accurate possible diff, but it's fast enough to run on every render, which matters more in practice.

question: Question 4. Why does React need keys in lists, and what actually goes wrong when they're missing, unstable, or duplicated?
answer:
Keys give React a stable identity for each item in a list, independent of its position. Without them, React has no way to tell whether an item moved, was replaced, or is brand new — so it falls back to comparing children purely by their index in the array.

That fallback is where bugs come from. If you reorder, insert, or remove an item in the middle of a list that's keyed by index, React can end up reusing the wrong DOM node for the wrong data — you'll see things like form inputs keeping stale values, or animations firing on the wrong element, because React thought an existing node just changed its content instead of realizing an item moved.

Duplicate keys cause a different problem — React can't reliably distinguish the items anymore, so you get warnings in the console and unpredictable behavior about which instance actually updates.

The fix is to use something that's stable and unique to the data itself — a database id, a generated uuid, anything that stays attached to that particular item regardless of where it ends up in the array.
bonus: Using the array index as a key is fine only when the list is static and never reordered, filtered, or has items inserted in the middle — which in practice is a smaller set of cases than people assume.

question: Question 5. What actually happens under the hood when you type into a controlled input, and why does that make every keystroke trigger a re-render?
answer:
In a controlled input, the value prop comes from React state, and the onChange handler is what updates that state. So the actual sequence per keystroke is: the browser fires a native input event, React's synthetic event system wraps it, your onChange handler runs and calls setState, and that triggers a re-render that feeds the new value back into the input's value prop.

That's why controlled inputs re-render on every keystroke — the component owning that state re-runs its render function each time. For a single text field this is invisible, but if that state lives high up in a large component tree, every character typed can re-render far more than just the input.

Uncontrolled inputs skip that entire cycle — the DOM manages the input's value itself, and you only read it when you need to, usually through a ref. That trades away render-on-every-keystroke for giving up React's ability to reactively validate or transform the value as the user types.

A common middle ground for performance-sensitive forms is to keep inputs uncontrolled during typing and only sync to React state on blur or on submit, so you get native input performance without losing validation entirely.
bonus: Libraries like React Hook Form lean heavily into this trade-off — they keep most inputs uncontrolled internally and only trigger re-renders for the specific fields that actually need to reflect state changes, like validation errors.

question: Question 6. What does React.StrictMode actually do, and why do effects run twice in development because of it?
answer:
StrictMode doesn't render any visible UI — it's a wrapper component that opts a subtree into extra development-only checks meant to surface bugs before they show up in production.

One of the more surprising things it does in React 18 is intentionally mount, unmount, and remount every component in the subtree once on initial render, which means effects run, clean up, and run again immediately. That's not a bug — it's simulating what happens when a component gets removed and re-added later, for example by a cached navigation, so you notice early if your effect's cleanup function doesn't properly undo everything the effect set up.

StrictMode also detects things like unexpected side effects during rendering by intentionally double-invoking render functions and certain lifecycle-adjacent hooks, and it warns about deprecated APIs and legacy patterns like the old string ref API.

None of this happens in production builds — it's purely a development tool. If double effects break your app, that's usually a sign the effect's cleanup logic was incomplete, not that StrictMode is misbehaving.
bonus: A very common StrictMode surprise is a data-fetching effect that fires the request twice — the fix isn't to remove StrictMode, it's to make sure the effect's cleanup can actually cancel or ignore the first, now-stale request.

question: Question 7. Why do we need Fragments in React, and what problem do they actually solve?
answer:
Every component has to return a single root value from its render function — you can't return two sibling elements side by side. Before Fragments existed, the common workaround was wrapping siblings in an extra div, just so there'd be one root node.

The problem is that extra div isn't free — it shows up in the actual DOM, which can break CSS layouts that depend on direct parent-child relationships, like flexbox or grid, and it adds noise to the accessibility tree and browser inspector.

A Fragment lets a component return multiple children without introducing any actual DOM element — React just renders the children directly into the parent, with no wrapper at all. You can write it with the explicit Fragment import or with the empty angle bracket shorthand.

Fragments can also take a key prop, which matters specifically when you're rendering a list of grouped elements and need each group to have a stable identity for reconciliation, since the shorthand syntax doesn't support props like key.
bonus: If you ever need a key on a fragment, you have to use the explicit React.Fragment form — the empty-tag shorthand has no way to accept props at all.

question: Question 8. What are portals in React, and how do events behave when an element is rendered through one?
answer:
A portal lets you render a component's output into a DOM node that lives outside its parent's DOM hierarchy, while keeping it in the same place in the React component tree. You create one with createPortal, passing the children and the target DOM node to render into.

The classic use case is anything that needs to visually escape its container's boundaries — modals, tooltips, dropdown menus, toasts. If a modal is rendered deep inside a component with overflow hidden or a low z-index, a portal lets you render its actual markup as a direct child of the body element instead, avoiding clipping and stacking context problems entirely.

What's interesting is that even though the DOM node lives elsewhere, the React tree position doesn't change. Events that bubble up from inside the portal still bubble up through the React component tree as if the portal weren't there, not through its new DOM location. So a click inside a modal can still be caught by an onClick handler on a logical ancestor in your component tree, even though that ancestor isn't an actual DOM ancestor anymore.

Context also flows through normally across a portal boundary, since context follows the React tree, not the DOM tree.
bonus: This event-bubbling behavior is why you sometimes see a portal-rendered dropdown accidentally trigger a click-outside handler on its logical parent — the event bubbles through React's tree, not the visual DOM structure.

question: Question 9. How does React batch state updates, and why can't you read the updated value immediately after calling setState?
answer:
When you call the state setter from useState, React doesn't immediately re-run your component and give you a new value on the next line — it schedules an update. The variable you have in that render's closure still holds the old value for the rest of that function call, because state updates take effect on the next render, not synchronously.

Batching means React can group multiple state updates that happen close together into a single re-render, instead of re-rendering once per update. If you call two different setters inside the same event handler, React applies both changes and re-renders once, rather than twice.

Before React 18, this batching only reliably happened inside React's own event handlers — updates inside a promise callback, a setTimeout, or a native event listener would each trigger their own separate render. React 18 introduced automatic batching, which extends that same behavior to essentially all state updates, regardless of where they're triggered from.

If you genuinely need to force separate renders — which is rare — you can wrap an update in flushSync to opt out of batching for that specific call.
bonus: This is also why the functional updater form, passing a function like previous value plus one instead of a plain value, matters when you call the setter multiple times in a row — it guarantees each call operates on the latest pending state rather than the value captured at render time.

question: Question 10. How do you think about cleanup functions and dependency arrays in useEffect, and what bugs come from getting them wrong?
answer:
The dependency array tells React when it's safe to skip re-running an effect — it compares each value in the array to its previous render using a simple equality check, and only re-runs the effect if something actually changed.

The cleanup function you return from an effect runs before the effect runs again, and also when the component unmounts. So for a subscription or a timer, the mental model is: on every render where a dependency changed, tear down the old one, then set up the new one.

The most common bug is an incomplete dependency array — leaving out a value the effect actually uses. This creates a stale closure, where the effect keeps referencing an old version of a variable from an earlier render, because it was never told to re-run when that variable changed. Lint rules like the exhaustive-deps rule exist specifically to catch this.

The opposite mistake is including something in the dependency array that changes on every render, like a new object or function created inline — that turns the effect into something that fires on every single render, defeating the point of the dependency array entirely.
bonus: A useful debugging habit is asking, for every effect, what would happen if this ran on every render with no dependency array at all — if the answer is chaos, that tells you the effect is doing something dependencies alone can't fully solve, and you might need a ref or a different pattern.

question: Question 11. When do you reach for useRef instead of useState, and what's actually different about how they work?
answer:
useState triggers a re-render whenever you update it — that's the whole point, it's for values that are part of what gets rendered. useRef gives you a mutable container that persists across renders, but changing it does not trigger a re-render at all.

I reach for useRef when I need to keep track of something the component needs internally, but that something isn't part of the visual output — a previous value for comparison, a timer id so I can clear it later, an instance of a third-party library, or a flag to avoid running logic twice.

The other big use case is holding a reference to an actual DOM node, so you can call imperative methods on it directly — focusing an input, measuring an element's size, or scrolling something into view. React attaches the real DOM node to ref.current after the browser has painted.

A rule I follow is: if changing this value should visibly affect what's rendered, it belongs in state. If it's bookkeeping that the component needs but the user never sees reflected directly, it belongs in a ref.
bonus: Mutating a ref during render itself is something to avoid — refs are meant to be read and written in effects or event handlers, not while the component function is actively computing its output.

question: Question 12. When do useMemo and useCallback actually help performance, and when are they just adding overhead for nothing?
answer:
Both hooks exist to preserve referential stability between renders — useMemo caches a computed value, useCallback caches a function reference, and both skip recalculating unless their dependencies changed.

They genuinely help in two situations. First, when the computation itself is expensive enough that redoing it on every render would be noticeably slow — filtering or transforming a large array, for example. Second, when the stable reference is being passed to something that cares about referential equality, most commonly a child component wrapped in React.memo, or another hook's dependency array where you want to avoid triggering extra effect runs.

Where they don't help is the common case people misuse them for — wrapping every function and every derived value defensively, assuming it's always a performance win. Comparing dependencies still costs something, storing the cached value costs memory, and if nothing downstream actually cares about referential identity, you've added complexity for a benefit that never materializes. For a component that renders quickly and isn't passed to a memoized child, the memoization overhead can be a net loss.

My default is to write plain functions and values first, and only introduce useMemo or useCallback after I've actually measured a re-render problem tied to reference identity or a genuinely expensive computation.
bonus: The React team has said plainly that these hooks are an escape hatch for specific performance problems, not something to reach for by default — which is a big part of the motivation behind the React Compiler doing this automatically.

question: Question 13. When do you prefer useReducer over useState, and what does it actually buy you?
answer:
useReducer centralizes state updates into a single function — the reducer — that takes the current state and an action, and returns the next state. Instead of scattering setter calls throughout a component, every possible state transition is described in one place.

I reach for it when a piece of state has several related fields that change together, or when the next state genuinely depends on the action taken rather than just being an independent overwrite. A form with several fields, validation state, and submission status is a good example — modeling that as a handful of separate useState calls gets messy fast, because you end up manually keeping them in sync.

It also makes testing and reasoning easier, because a reducer is a pure function — given the same state and action, you always get the same result, with no hidden component context involved. You can unit test it completely separately from any rendering.

For genuinely simple, independent pieces of state — a boolean toggle, a single text value — useReducer is usually overkill. It shines specifically when the update logic itself has some complexity worth naming and isolating.
bonus: useReducer is essentially a scaled-down version of the same pattern Redux is built around — actions and a pure reducer function — which is why moving from useReducer to Redux later, if a project outgrows local state, usually isn't a big conceptual jump.

question: Question 14. What makes a custom hook well designed, versus one that just moves the mess somewhere else?
answer:
A good custom hook has a clear, narrow responsibility and hides its internal mechanics behind a small, intention-revealing return value. Something like useDebouncedValue or useLocalStorage tells you exactly what it does just from the name, without needing to read its implementation.

I pay attention to what the hook returns. If it has more than two or three closely related values, I usually return an object with named properties instead of an array, because callers benefit from named destructuring and it's easier to add new fields later without breaking every call site — versus an array where the order carries all the meaning.

A well-designed hook also doesn't leak implementation details it doesn't need to. If it wraps a subscription or a timer internally, the component using it shouldn't need to know that, or manage cleanup itself — the hook handles its own effects and cleanup completely.

Composability matters too — hooks calling other hooks is completely normal and often the whole point, letting you build something like useAuthenticatedFetch out of a simpler useFetch plus a useAuth hook, rather than duplicating logic.

The failure mode I watch for is a hook that just externalizes a component's mess without actually simplifying anything — if using the hook requires the caller to still understand its internals to use it correctly, it hasn't really abstracted anything.
bonus: Naming a hook's return values consistently across your codebase — always calling a loading flag isLoading, for example — makes hooks compose more predictably when several of them show up in the same component.

question: Question 15. Walk me through a concrete case where using useEffect instead of useLayoutEffect causes a visible flicker.
answer:
Say you're building a tooltip that needs to measure its own size after rendering, to decide whether it should flip to the other side of the target element so it doesn't overflow the viewport. You render the tooltip, measure it in an effect, and then update its position based on that measurement.

If you do that measurement and reposition inside useEffect, the sequence is: React commits the DOM changes, the browser paints the tooltip at its initial, wrong position, and only after that paint does your effect run, measure, and correct the position. The user sees a flash of the tooltip in the wrong place before it jumps to the correct one.

useLayoutEffect runs synchronously after the DOM is updated but before the browser paints anything. So the same measure-and-reposition logic happens, but it happens before the user ever sees a frame — the browser paints the corrected position on the very first paint, with no visible jump.

The trade-off is that useLayoutEffect blocks the browser from painting until it finishes, so if you use it for something slow, you've turned a smooth update into a janky one. That's why it's reserved specifically for synchronous DOM reads and writes that need to happen before paint — measuring layout, and adjusting scroll position are the classic cases — and useEffect stays the default for everything else, including fetching data and setting up subscriptions.
bonus: A quick way to decide between them: if skipping straight to useEffect would cause something the user can visibly see flicker or jump, that's the signal to use useLayoutEffect instead.

question: Question 16. Why can't you call hooks conditionally or inside loops, and how does React even know which piece of state belongs to which useState call?
answer:
React doesn't track hook state by name — there's no string identifier tying a particular useState call to its stored value. Instead, React relies entirely on call order. Internally, each component instance keeps something like a linked list of hook state, and every time the component renders, React walks through that list in the exact order the hooks were called, matching the first useState call in this render to the first one from the previous render, the second to the second, and so on.

That's the entire reason for the rule that hooks must always be called in the same order, every render, with no conditionals or loops wrapping them. If you call useState conditionally, and that condition changes between renders, the third hook call in one render might line up with a completely different hook call from the previous render — React would attach the wrong stored state to the wrong hook, silently corrupting things or throwing an error.

The fix is always to keep the hook call unconditional, and put the conditional logic inside the hook instead — call useState every time, and use an if statement to decide what to do with the value.

This is also exactly why hooks only work in function components and other hooks, not in regular functions called from inside a component — React's tracking is tied to the component's own render call, not to the call site generically.
bonus: The eslint-plugin-react-hooks rule that enforces this catches most violations automatically, which is part of why it's treated as a near-mandatory lint rule rather than a style preference.

question: Question 17. What are the performance implications of useContext, and how do you avoid a context update re-rendering half the app?
answer:
Every component that calls useContext for a given context subscribes to that context's value. When the value passed to the corresponding Provider changes, every one of those consuming components re-renders — regardless of whether that specific component actually cares about the part of the value that changed.

This becomes a real problem when a context holds several unrelated pieces of state bundled into one object, and a component only reads one field from it. A change to any field re-renders every consumer, even the ones only interested in a field that didn't change.

The first fix is splitting one large context into several smaller, more focused ones, so a component only subscribes to the pieces it actually needs. A second, common pattern is separating state and setter functions into two different contexts — a lot of consumers only need the setter, which never changes, so they can avoid re-rendering entirely when the state itself updates.

Another trick is memoizing the value object passed to the Provider itself with useMemo, since passing a freshly created object literal on every render of the provider defeats even React.memo on the consumers, because the object reference changes every time regardless of whether the actual data did.
bonus: If context re-renders become a real bottleneck despite splitting things up, that's usually the signal to move that state into an external store with useSyncExternalStore or a small state library instead, since context was never designed as a high-frequency update mechanism.

question: Question 18. What problem do useTransition and useDeferredValue solve, and how are they different from each other?
answer:
Both exist to let you deprioritize a piece of rendering work so it doesn't block more urgent updates, like text appearing in an input as the user types. Without them, a slow re-render triggered by fast typing can make the whole page feel like it's lagging behind every keystroke.

useTransition wraps a state update itself, marking it as a low-priority transition. You get back a pending flag and a function to wrap the update in. React renders the urgent update — like the input's own value — immediately, and works on the transition update in the background, interrupting it if something more urgent comes in before it finishes.

useDeferredValue works on a value you already have, rather than the update that produced it. You give it a fast-changing value, like a raw search query, and it gives you back a version of that value that lags behind slightly when React is busy, letting expensive downstream rendering — like a big filtered list — catch up without blocking the input.

In practice, you use useTransition when you control the state update that's causing the slow render, and useDeferredValue when you're consuming a value from somewhere you don't control, like a prop, and just want to soften how quickly downstream work reacts to it.
bonus: Both rely on React 18's concurrent rendering — the ability to pause, interrupt, and resume rendering work — which is also what makes it safe for React to throw away a half-finished low-priority render instead of finishing it first.

question: Question 19. Beyond the basic idea of lifting state to a common parent, how do you decide where state should actually live in a larger component tree?
answer:
The instinct a lot of people have is to lift state as high as possible, just in case something else needs it later. In a small app that's harmless, but in a larger tree it quietly creates a performance and maintainability problem, because every state update re-renders that high-up component and, by default, every descendant beneath it.

The principle I follow is state colocation — state should live as close as possible to the components that actually use it, and only move upward when a second component genuinely needs to read or change it too. If only one button's open or closed state matters, that state belongs inside that button's own component, not hoisted up to a page-level component just because it seemed like the safer default.

When you do need to lift state because two siblings share it, it's worth asking whether the shared piece is really the whole state object, or just one field. Sometimes lifting only the specific value that's shared, while keeping the rest local to each component, avoids re-rendering unrelated siblings entirely.

A useful check is asking, after lifting some state up, which components between the new state's owner and the components that actually read it will now re-render unnecessarily — if that list is long, it's often a sign the state should be split rather than fully centralized.
bonus: This is also the underlying idea behind wrapping a slow, unrelated piece of UI in its own component with children passed through — it stops a parent's state change from forcing that unrelated subtree to re-render just because it happens to live nearby in the tree.

question: Question 20. What are the real trade-offs of using Context for shared state, beyond just being built in?
answer:
Context solves the specific problem of passing a value down through many layers without threading it through every intermediate component's props. That's a real win for genuinely global, rarely-changing values — theme, locale, the current authenticated user.

The trade-off is that Context wasn't designed as a general-purpose state management tool, and it shows in a few specific ways. It has no built-in mechanism for selecting just part of a value — every consumer re-renders on any change to the context value, which I've run into as a real bottleneck when a context holds frequently changing state read by many components. It also has no built-in way to update state from outside React's render tree, and no equivalent of middleware, devtools, or time-travel debugging that dedicated state libraries offer.

Context also composes awkwardly when you have several independent pieces of global state — you end up either bundling them into one object, which reintroduces the over-broad re-render problem, or nesting several separate providers, which adds visible wrapper nesting at the root of your app.

So the honest framing is that Context is a dependency-injection mechanism first, and a state management tool second. It's a great fit for values that change rarely and are read widely, and a poor fit for state that changes often and is read selectively.
bonus: A good gut check is asking how often the value changes relative to how many components read it — infrequent changes with wide readership is Context's sweet spot, frequent changes with wide readership is where it usually breaks down.

question: Question 21. Besides Context, what are some other ways to avoid deep prop drilling?
answer:
Context is the answer people reach for first, but composition often solves the same problem without introducing a context at all. Instead of passing a value down through five layers of components that don't actually use it, you can restructure so the component that has the value directly renders the component that needs it, passing it as a child or a prop at that point, rather than routing it through unrelated intermediate components.

A common version of this is the slot pattern — instead of a Page component receiving a bunch of data and passing pieces of it down into a Sidebar and a Header that it renders internally, the parent that actually owns the data renders Sidebar and Header directly as children of Page, each getting exactly the props they need, with Page itself never touching that data.

Component composition through the children prop works similarly — a generic Layout component doesn't need to know about user data at all if the page using it just passes the user-specific pieces as children, already fully formed.

For cases where the drilled value doesn't change and is read in many unrelated places, a small custom hook that reads from a shared source — even something as simple as a module-level store outside React entirely — can also sidestep the problem without any prop passing at all.
bonus: Reaching for restructuring before reaching for Context is often the better first instinct — Context solves the symptom, but a lot of prop drilling is actually a sign the component tree's structure doesn't match how the data naturally flows.

question: Question 22. How do you decide when a project actually needs a dedicated state library instead of just Context and useReducer?
answer:
I look at three things — update frequency, how selective components need to be about what they read, and how much tooling the team actually needs around the state.

If the shared state updates often and is read by many components, but each component only cares about a slice of it, a library like Zustand, Jotai, or Redux Toolkit gives you selective subscriptions out of the box — a component only re-renders when the specific slice it reads changes, which Context can't do without a lot of manual splitting.

If the app needs things like time-travel debugging, middleware for logging or persistence, or a strict, testable pattern for a large team to follow consistently, Redux's structure earns its overhead — the explicitness that feels like boilerplate in a small app becomes valuable discipline in a large one.

For state that's genuinely simple and doesn't change often — feature flags, theme, the current user — Context plus useReducer is usually enough, and reaching for a library adds a dependency and a mental model the team has to maintain for no real benefit.

My rule of thumb is to start with Context and local state, and only introduce a state library once I can point to a concrete problem it solves — a real re-render bottleneck, or a genuine need for the tooling — rather than adopting one preemptively.
bonus: It's worth separating this decision from server state entirely — a lot of projects that think they need Redux actually just need a data-fetching library like TanStack Query, because most of what they're storing is really cached server data, not true client state.

question: Question 23. What's the actual difference between server state and client state, and why does treating them the same cause bugs?
answer:
Client state is data your application owns outright — a form's current input, whether a modal is open, which tab is selected. It only exists in memory, and your component is the single source of truth for it.

Server state is data that actually lives somewhere else — a database, another user's actions, a service you don't control — and your app is just holding a cached copy of it at a point in time. That copy can go stale the moment someone else changes the underlying data, which is a category of problem client state simply doesn't have.

When you store server data in a plain useState and treat it like client state, you inherit all of server state's real problems without any of the tools designed to handle them — you have to manually track loading and error states, manually decide when to refetch, manually deduplicate requests if the same data is needed in two places, and manually handle what happens if a request is still in flight when the component unmounts.

Libraries built for server state, like TanStack Query or SWR, exist specifically to handle that different set of problems — caching, background refetching, deduplication, and invalidation — as a bounded, mostly solved thing, so your component code just declares what data it needs, not how to manage its lifecycle.
bonus: A good test for which bucket a piece of state belongs to is asking who else could change it. If the answer is only this component, right now, it's client state. If the answer involves another request, another user, or the passage of time, it's server state.

question: Question 24. What are the main data fetching patterns in React, and what's actually different about fetch-on-render versus render-as-you-fetch?
answer:
Fetch-on-render is the pattern most people write first — a component mounts, an effect fires, the fetch starts only once the component has already rendered. The downside is a visible delay: render, then wait, then fetch, then render again with data. If a parent and child both fetch independently this way, you get a waterfall — the child's fetch doesn't even start until the parent has rendered and mounted, even though nothing about the child's data actually depended on the parent finishing first.

Fetch-then-render flips the order — you kick off the fetch before rendering anything, typically in a route loader or at the top of a navigation, and only render the component once the data has arrived. This avoids the waterfall for that particular fetch, but if there's nothing to show while waiting, the user just sees a blank screen or a full-page spinner instead.

Render-as-you-fetch starts the fetch immediately, at the same time as starting to render, and lets the component tree render around the data as it becomes available — showing skeletons or fallbacks for the parts still loading via Suspense, while parts that don't depend on that data render immediately. This is the pattern React's own Suspense-based data fetching and frameworks like Next.js's server components lean into, because it starts fetching as early as possible without blocking everything else from rendering.

In practice, the biggest real-world win is just starting fetches as early as possible and in parallel wherever they don't actually depend on each other, regardless of which of these patterns you're using.
bonus: Request waterfalls are one of the most common, and most fixable, performance problems in React apps — a quick audit of the network tab, looking for requests that only start after a previous one finishes for no real reason, usually finds several.

question: Question 25. How do you implement optimistic updates, and what happens when the underlying request fails?
answer:
An optimistic update means you update the UI to reflect the expected result of an action immediately, before the server has actually confirmed it — liking a post shows the like right away, instead of waiting for the network round trip to finish first.

The implementation is usually: capture the current state before you change anything, apply the expected new state immediately, fire off the actual request in the background, and then either leave the optimistic state in place if the request succeeds, or roll back to the captured previous state if it fails.

The tricky part is the failure path. Rolling back isn't just visually reverting — you often need to show the user why it failed, since silently undoing something they just did without explanation is confusing. And if multiple optimistic updates can happen in quick succession, you need to be careful that a later failure doesn't roll back a change that a different, still-pending update already built on top of.

React has a built-in useOptimistic hook now specifically for this pattern — you give it the current state and a function describing how an action should update it optimistically, and it manages showing that optimistic value until the real update resolves, falling back automatically if it doesn't.
bonus: Optimistic updates work best for actions that almost always succeed, like a like button or a toggle — for anything with a meaningful failure rate, like a payment, the jarring rollback experience often isn't worth the perceived speed gain.

question: Question 26. What's the classic mistake with derived state in React, and how do you avoid it?
answer:
The mistake is copying a value that can already be computed from existing props or state into its own separate state variable, usually by syncing it with a useEffect. Something like taking a list prop and copying it into local state just to filter it, then using an effect to keep that copy updated whenever the prop changes.

The problem is that this creates two sources of truth for the same information, and an effect that syncs one to the other. The effect runs after render, so there's a moment where the UI is showing outdated derived data, and it's easy for the sync logic to miss a case and let the two fall out of alignment entirely. It also means an extra unnecessary render happens every time — one for the prop update, one for the effect updating the copied state in response.

The fix is almost always to just calculate the derived value directly during render, instead of storing it separately at all. If filtering a large list is genuinely expensive, wrapping that calculation in useMemo keeps it fast without needing a second piece of state or an effect.

The one legitimate case where you do want to reset local state based on a prop is when a component instance is conceptually restarting — for example a form resetting when it's now editing a different record. Even then, the recommended fix isn't an effect — it's passing a key on the component tied to the record's id, so React just unmounts and remounts the component with fresh state, rather than trying to sync state to a prop from inside.
bonus: The React docs have an entire page specifically about avoiding this pattern, called you might not need an effect — it's worth reading end to end, since this single mistake accounts for a surprising share of hard-to-debug state bugs.

question: Question 27. What are the common pitfalls with React.memo, and when does it actually fail to prevent a re-render?
answer:
React.memo skips re-rendering a component if its props are shallowly equal to the previous render's props — meaning it compares each prop with a reference equality check, not a deep comparison. That word shallow is where most of the pitfalls come from.

If a parent passes an inline object, array, or function as a prop, a new one is created on every single render of the parent, even if its contents are identical. React.memo's shallow comparison sees a different reference and re-renders the child anyway, completely defeating the memoization. This is why memoizing a component without also stabilizing the props it receives, usually with useMemo or useCallback in the parent, often accomplishes nothing.

Another pitfall is the children prop specifically — if a memoized component receives JSX as children from a parent that re-renders, that JSX is a freshly created element on every parent render too, so the memoized component re-renders regardless of its other props staying the same.

React.memo does accept a second argument, a custom comparison function, for cases where you genuinely need more control than shallow equality gives you — but reaching for that is a sign worth double-checking, since a custom comparison that's wrong can cause a component to silently miss updates it should have rendered for.
bonus: Before reaching for React.memo, it's worth checking whether the re-render is actually slow enough to matter — memoizing a component that renders in under a millisecond just adds a comparison cost on every render for no measurable benefit.

question: Question 28. What are the actual triggers that cause a React component to re-render?
answer:
There are really three root causes, and everything else is a variation on one of them. The first is the component's own state changing — calling a state setter, even with the same value in some cases, schedules a re-render of that component.

The second is a parent re-rendering. By default, when a component re-renders, React re-renders every child underneath it too, regardless of whether that child's own props actually changed — React still has to run the child's render function to compute its new element tree and compare it, even if the result ends up being identical to before.

The third is a context value changing, which re-renders every component subscribed to that context via useContext, independent of where those components sit in the tree relative to the Provider.

A subtlety worth knowing is that calling a state setter with a value that's identical to the current one, using an Object.is style comparison, lets React bail out of re-rendering that specific component — but that bail-out only applies to the component that owns the state itself, not automatically to its children, unless they're wrapped in something like React.memo.
bonus: Understanding that a parent re-rendering its children by default is the standard, un-optimized behavior — not a bug — is one of those things that, once it clicks, makes almost every other React performance topic make more sense.

question: Question 29. What problem does the React Compiler solve, and does it mean we no longer need useMemo and useCallback?
answer:
The React Compiler analyzes your component code at build time and automatically inserts the equivalent of memoization — for values, for callback functions, and effectively for whole subtrees of JSX — wherever it determines it's safe and beneficial to do so, without you writing useMemo or useCallback by hand.

The problem it's solving is that manual memoization is easy to get wrong in exactly the ways I mentioned earlier — forgetting to memoize a value passed to a memoized child, memoizing something that never actually needed it, or getting a dependency array wrong. The compiler can, in principle, get this right consistently because it can statically analyze exactly what a piece of code depends on, rather than relying on a developer to track it by hand.

In practice, this doesn't mean useMemo and useCallback disappear from the language, or that you should never reach for them again. The compiler handles the common cases automatically, but there are still situations, like intentionally opting a specific value out of recalculation for reasons the compiler can't infer, where manual memoization still has a place. It's also an opt-in build step, so a project only gets these benefits once it's actually enabled and its rules are followed, like the existing rules of hooks.

The bigger shift is philosophical — instead of treating manual memoization as a routine performance technique to reach for by default, the expectation is that most components can just be written in the most natural, unoptimized way, and the compiler handles making that fast.
bonus: The compiler still relies on your code actually following the rules of React — no mutating state directly, no impure render logic — because those same rules are what let it safely reason about what can be cached without changing behavior.

question: Question 30. How do you design Suspense boundaries when code splitting with lazy, and what goes wrong if you get the boundary placement wrong?
answer:
React.lazy lets a component be loaded on demand, but it can only work paired with a Suspense boundary somewhere above it in the tree, which is what actually shows a fallback while the lazy component's code is still downloading.

Where you place that boundary matters a lot. If you wrap your entire app in one single Suspense boundary at the root, then loading any single lazy piece — even something small, deep in the tree — shows the loading fallback for the entire page, throwing away everything else that was already rendered and usable. That's a jarring experience for something that should be a small, localized loading state.

The better approach is usually several smaller, more targeted Suspense boundaries, placed close to the actual lazy components — one around a route's main content, maybe another around a heavy widget within that route that loads independently. That way, loading one part shows a fallback for just that part, while the rest of the page stays interactive.

The other thing to watch for is a boundary placed too low, wrapping only the lazy component itself with nothing else nearby — this can lead to a page that shows several tiny, disconnected loading spinners popping in and out as different pieces resolve at different times, which often looks worse than one well-placed loading state for a whole section.
bonus: Preloading matters just as much as boundary placement — triggering the dynamic import for a route on hover or on link focus, before the user actually clicks, can make a lazy-loaded page feel like it loaded instantly.

question: Question 31. What actually happens under the hood in a virtualized list, and what makes dynamic row heights hard?
answer:
A virtualized list only renders the DOM nodes for items currently visible in the viewport, plus a small buffer of extra items just outside it, called overscan, so scrolling doesn't show a flash of empty space while new items render in.

To make this work, the list needs to know the total scrollable height up front, even though most of the items aren't actually rendered — it does this by tracking each item's height, or estimating it, and using that to compute where the scrollbar should be and which items should currently be visible based on the scroll position.

When every item has a fixed, known height, this is straightforward — height times index gives you the position of any item without rendering it. Dynamic heights, where each item's height depends on its own content, break that shortcut. The list has to either measure items after they've rendered once and cache that measurement, or estimate a height before rendering and correct the total scroll height as real measurements come in — which can cause the scrollbar or scroll position to visibly jump slightly as estimates get corrected.

Libraries like react-virtual and react-window handle this measurement and caching for you, but it's worth understanding that dynamic-height virtualization is fundamentally a harder problem than fixed-height virtualization, which is why it's more prone to subtle visual glitches like content jumping during fast scrolling.
bonus: Overscan amount is a real tuning knob — too little and users see blank flashes while scrolling fast, too much and you lose a chunk of the performance benefit virtualization was supposed to give you in the first place.

question: Question 32. How do you use the React DevTools Profiler to actually find the source of a performance problem?
answer:
I start a profiling recording, perform the interaction that feels slow — typing in a search box, opening a panel, whatever it is — and stop the recording. That gives me a series of commits, each one representing a point where React actually re-rendered something in response to a state or props change.

For each commit, the flame graph shows every component that rendered during that commit, with the width of each bar representing how long it took relative to the rest. Wide bars are the components actually costing time — that's usually where I look first, rather than guessing.

The ranked chart view is often more useful for finding the actual worst offender — it lists every component in that commit sorted by render duration, so instead of visually scanning a tree, you get a straight list of what took the longest.

What I'm specifically looking for is components that rendered but shouldn't have — the Profiler can highlight why a component re-rendered, showing which prop or state or context changed and triggered it. If a component re-rendered but none of its actual displayed output changed as a result, that's a wasted render, and it's usually fixable with memoization, moving state down, or splitting a context — but only after the profiler has told me specifically which component and which trigger to target.
bonus: A profiling session recorded once, before making any change, and compared against one recorded after, is the only reliable way to confirm an optimization actually helped — eyeballing whether something feels faster is not a substitute for comparing real commit durations.

question: Question 33. What does concurrent actually mean in React 18, and how is it different from just being asynchronous?
answer:
Concurrent doesn't mean React runs code on multiple threads at the same time — JavaScript in the browser is still single-threaded. What it means is that React can start rendering an update, pause partway through if something more urgent comes in, work on the urgent update instead, and then either resume or discard the paused work.

This is different from the pre-concurrent model, where once React started rendering an update, it ran that render to completion synchronously before handling anything else, even if a more important update — like responding to a keystroke — was waiting the whole time.

Time slicing is the specific mechanism that makes this possible — React breaks rendering work for a low-priority update into small chunks, and after each chunk, checks whether something more urgent needs the main thread before continuing with the next chunk. This is what makes features like useTransition possible — the transition's rendering work happens in these interruptible slices, so a fast, urgent update like text appearing in an input doesn't have to wait behind it.

The reason this matters practically is that it changes what a slow render actually costs you. In the old model, a slow render blocked everything, including the browser's ability to respond to input, until it finished. In the concurrent model, that same slow render can be interrupted, so the app stays responsive even while a lower-priority part of the UI is still catching up.
bonus: This is also why concurrent features require your rendering to be free of side effects during render — if React can pause, throw away, and restart a render, any side effect that ran during that discarded attempt would have run for nothing, or worse, run twice.

question: Question 34. Beyond splitting contexts, what more advanced techniques help avoid a context update re-rendering components that don't actually care?
answer:
Splitting a context into smaller pieces helps, but it only goes so far — if a single piece of state genuinely needs to be read selectively by different components, at some point splitting contexts further just turns into managing dozens of tiny providers.

One technique is moving the shared state out of React's context entirely and into an external store, then subscribing to it with useSyncExternalStore. That hook lets a component subscribe to just the specific slice of an external store it cares about, and only re-render when that specific slice actually changes — which is exactly the kind of selective subscription plain Context can't do on its own. This is effectively how libraries like Zustand and Redux avoid the broad-re-render problem under the hood.

Another pattern, sometimes called context selectors, wraps a context's value access in a way that individual components subscribe only to a computed piece of it, using a library like use-context-selector, rather than the whole value object.

A more structural fix, when it's an option, is to stop the re-render from being a problem in the first place — wrapping the components that read unrelated props in React.memo, and passing children down through composition, so that even though a context change causes some re-render, the components that don't consume that particular context skip re-rendering because their own props genuinely didn't change.
bonus: It's worth periodically re-checking whether a piece of state even belongs in Context at all — a lot of context re-render problems disappear entirely once the state in question is recognized as belonging in a dedicated store instead.

question: Question 35. How does the compound components pattern work, and what problem is it solving?
answer:
Compound components are a set of components designed to be used together, sharing implicit state between them, without the parent needing to pass that state down through explicit props on every child. Something like a Tabs component made up of Tabs, Tabs.List, Tabs.Tab, and Tabs.Panel, where you compose them as JSX children rather than configuring everything through one big prop object on Tabs itself.

The implementation almost always relies on Context. The outer component creates the shared state — which tab is currently active — and provides it through a context, and the inner pieces, like an individual Tab, read from that context to know whether they're the active one and to trigger a change when clicked.

What this buys you is a much more flexible, declarative API from the consumer's side. Instead of passing an array of tab configuration objects into a single component, the consumer can freely rearrange, conditionally render, or insert arbitrary content between the pieces, because they're just regular JSX children being composed, not data being interpreted from a config object.

The trade-off is that the pieces are coupled to each other implicitly — Tabs.Tab genuinely doesn't work correctly outside of a Tabs.List, which needs to be communicated clearly, usually with a runtime error if the required context isn't found, since there's no way to enforce that coupling at the type level in plain JavaScript.
bonus: This pattern is exactly how a lot of headless UI libraries like Radix structure their accordion, tabs, and menu components — it's worth reading their source for a concrete, well-tested example of the pattern in practice.

question: Question 36. Why did hooks mostly replace the render props pattern, and are there cases where render props still make sense?
answer:
Render props solved a real problem before hooks existed — sharing stateful logic between components when you couldn't just extract a function, because that logic needed to actually hook into a component's lifecycle. The pattern was a component that took a function as a prop, usually as children, and called that function with whatever state or behavior it managed internally, letting the caller render whatever they wanted with it.

The practical downside was nesting. Combining several pieces of shared logic through render props meant wrapping components several layers deep, each one only there to unlock access to some shared behavior, which made the JSX tree harder to read and made the actual component hierarchy in DevTools noisier than the visual structure warranted.

Hooks solve the same underlying problem — sharing stateful logic — without needing a wrapper component or the associated nesting at all. A custom hook can encapsulate exactly the same logic a render-prop component would have, and a component just calls it directly, with no extra layer in the tree.

Render props haven't disappeared entirely — they still make sense specifically when the thing being shared is tied to markup or rendering itself, not just to stateful logic. A component that needs to control exactly when and how something renders, rather than just handing back some values, is still a legitimate case — some virtualization and drag-and-drop libraries still use this shape for exactly that reason.
bonus: A useful test is asking whether the shared thing is behavior, which you should extract as a hook, or rendering control, which is when a render prop, or an equivalent as prop pattern, still earns its place.

question: Question 37. Why did hooks replace most use cases for higher-order components, and where do HOCs still hold up?
answer:
A higher-order component is a function that takes a component and returns a new one, usually wrapping it with some extra props or behavior — connecting it to a store, or injecting some shared data. Like render props, they were the standard way to share cross-cutting logic before hooks existed.

The practical problems were similar too, plus a couple that were specific to HOCs. Composing several HOCs around one component created deeply nested wrapper components in the tree, which showed up as confusing, hard-to-read component names in DevTools. Prop naming collisions were a real issue too — if two different HOCs both wanted to inject a prop called data, you'd get a silent conflict. And forwarding refs through a HOC wrapper required extra, easy-to-forget boilerplate using forwardRef, since the wrapper itself sat between the caller and the actual underlying component.

Hooks sidestep essentially all of this — a custom hook doesn't wrap anything in the tree, doesn't introduce naming collisions since you choose what to call the returned values yourself, and has no ref-forwarding problem because there's no extra component in between at all.

HOCs still make sense specifically when you genuinely need to intercept or alter how a component is used at the component level itself, not just inject some data into it — things like a routing library's older wrapper-based patterns, or a HOC that conditionally renders an entirely different fallback component based on some check that happens before the wrapped component ever mounts.
bonus: If you find yourself reaching for a HOC today, it's worth first asking whether a hook plus normal composition through children can achieve the same result — in most modern codebases, the answer is yes.

question: Question 38. What is a headless component, and what does separating logic from rendering actually buy a team?
answer:
A headless component, or headless hook, provides all the behavior and state management a UI element needs — for example, all the logic a dropdown needs around open state, keyboard navigation, and focus management — without rendering any actual markup or styling of its own. You bring your own markup and styling, and wire it up to the values and handlers the headless piece gives you.

The motivation is that a huge amount of the hard, easy-to-get-wrong work in a component like a combobox or a date picker is behavioral, not visual — correct keyboard navigation, correct focus trapping, correct aria attributes for screen readers, correct behavior when items are added or removed dynamically. That logic is genuinely difficult to get fully right, and it's almost entirely independent of what the component actually looks like.

Separating it out means a design system can reuse the exact same, well-tested behavioral logic across every visual variant of a component the product needs, without re-solving the accessibility and interaction details each time a new visual treatment is needed. Libraries like Downshift, React Aria, and Radix's primitives are built around exactly this separation.

The trade-off is that you're writing more of the actual markup and styling yourself, rather than getting a fully styled component out of the box — which is a reasonable cost when your team has its own design system, and a worse fit when you just want something that looks reasonable with minimal setup.
bonus: Accessibility is probably the single biggest reason this pattern has become popular — encoding correct aria and keyboard behavior once, in a library many teams share, produces far more consistently accessible components than every team re-implementing that logic themselves.

question: Question 39. How do you decide between designing a component around composition, using children and slots, versus a large configuration prop API?
answer:
A configuration-driven component exposes its variations through props — a title prop, an icon prop, a variant prop, maybe a dozen boolean flags for different optional pieces. It's easy to use for the common cases, because the consumer just passes data and gets a result, without needing to know anything about the component's internal structure.

The problem shows up as the component needs to support more variation. Every new combination of needs tends to add another prop, and eventually you end up with a component that has thirty loosely related props, many of which only make sense in combination with certain others, and the internal implementation is full of conditionals trying to handle every combination correctly.

A composition-based design instead exposes structural pieces as children or named slots — a Card component that just renders whatever you put inside it, maybe with a few designated regions like a header slot and a footer slot, rather than a title prop and a footer prop with fixed behavior. This scales much better to unanticipated needs, because the consumer can put arbitrary content and behavior into a slot without the component's own code needing to know about that specific case in advance.

In practice, most well-designed component libraries land somewhere in between — configuration props for the small number of truly common variations, and composition through children or slots for anything more custom, so the common case stays simple while unusual cases aren't blocked entirely.
bonus: A good early warning sign that a component has outgrown pure configuration is when you find yourself adding a prop whose only job is to let the consumer override or disable something another prop already did — that's usually a sign a slot would let them just replace that piece directly instead.

question: Question 40. How do you think about error boundary granularity and recovery in a production app, beyond the basic idea of catching render errors?
answer:
A single error boundary around the entire app is better than nothing, but it means any unhandled error anywhere takes down the whole page with one fallback screen. In practice, I place boundaries at meaningful, independent sections of the UI — around a page's main content separately from its navigation, or around a specific widget that's more likely to fail, like one rendering data from a third-party integration — so a failure in one part doesn't take unrelated, working parts of the page down with it.

Catching the error is only half the job — I always wire the boundary's error handler up to whatever error reporting tool the team uses, like Sentry, so a caught error still gets logged with a stack trace and enough context to actually debug it later, rather than just silently showing a fallback and losing the signal that something broke.

Recovery matters too. A fallback UI that just says something went wrong, with no way to retry, often leaves a user stuck on a broken page until they manually reload. The common fix is giving the boundary a reset mechanism — either changing the boundary's key prop to force React to unmount and remount the whole subtree fresh, or using a library like react-error-boundary, which provides a reset function you can wire up to a retry button.

It's also worth remembering error boundaries don't catch everything — they don't catch errors inside event handlers, inside asynchronous code, or during server-side rendering itself, so a complete error-handling strategy still needs plain try and catch for those cases alongside the boundaries.
bonus: Testing that your fallback UI actually renders correctly is easy to skip — deliberately throwing an error in development, behind a temporary debug flag, is a quick way to confirm the boundary and its reset flow actually work before you need them in production.

question: Question 41. If you're designing a reusable component for a library, how do you support both controlled and uncontrolled usage cleanly?
answer:
The convention most of the ecosystem follows is pairing a value prop with an onChange handler for controlled usage, and a defaultValue prop for uncontrolled usage — mirroring how native form elements themselves already work. If the consumer passes value, the component treats them as fully in charge of the state and just reports changes upward. If they don't pass value, the component manages its own internal state, initialized from defaultValue if it's given.

Internally, this usually means the component always has some internal state as a fallback, but decides on every render whether to actually use that internal state or defer to the externally provided value — often by checking whether the value prop is defined at all. A common pitfall is a component that's accidentally controlled some of the time and uncontrolled other times, for example if a consumer passes value as undefined initially and then starts passing a real value later — that switch mid-lifecycle causes a distinct warning in React, because it changes which side owns the state partway through the component's life.

Good library components also expose a way to opt out cleanly rather than silently misbehaving — throwing a clear warning if a consumer passes both value and defaultValue at once, since that combination is a sign of confusion about which mode they intend to use.

The underlying goal is flexibility without surprise — a consumer who just wants a quick, working component reaches for the uncontrolled mode with a sensible default, and a consumer who needs to synchronize the value with other state in their app reaches for the controlled mode, and both should feel like using the same component, not two different APIs bolted together.
bonus: This exact pattern is worth studying directly in how native inputs behave — checkboxes, radios, and text inputs all support both an uncontrolled default and a fully controlled mode, and library components that mimic that same convention feel immediately familiar to anyone who's used plain HTML forms.

question: Question 42. How do you structure component architecture in a React codebase once it grows past a handful of contributors?
answer:
The organizing principle I lean on is grouping by feature or domain rather than by technical type. Instead of one top-level folder of every component in the app, another of every hook, and another of every utility, each feature gets its own folder containing the components, hooks, and utilities specific to that feature, so working on one feature means working almost entirely within one folder.

Within a feature folder, I keep a clear boundary between what's meant to be used by other features and what's a private implementation detail. A small number of things — the feature's main exported component, maybe a hook other features are allowed to use — get exposed, usually through a single entry file, while the rest stays unreachable from outside the folder by convention, or enforced by lint rules restricting cross-feature imports to that entry point.

Truly shared things — a generic Button, a date formatting utility, a design system's primitives — live in a separate shared or common area, but I try to keep that area intentionally small. A common failure mode at scale is a shared folder that slowly absorbs things that are really only used by one feature, because it felt more discoverable there, until it becomes an unstructured dumping ground that everything quietly depends on.

Circular dependencies are the other thing I actively watch for as the codebase grows — two features importing from each other, even indirectly through a few hops, are a sign the boundary between them isn't actually where the code structure claims it is, and usually the fix is extracting the truly shared piece both depend on into its own place, rather than having either feature depend on the other.
bonus: A useful test for whether feature boundaries are actually holding is asking whether a new engineer could delete an entire feature folder and, aside from removing wherever it's rendered at the top level, have the rest of the app keep working — if deleting one feature folder breaks unrelated features, the boundaries aren't real yet.

question: Question 43. What's the core philosophy behind React Testing Library, and how does it change the way you write component tests?
answer:
React Testing Library's guiding principle is that tests should resemble how a real user interacts with the app, rather than reaching into a component's internal implementation. The library's own tagline puts it directly — the more your tests resemble the way your software is actually used, the more confidence they give you.

In practice, this means you don't query the DOM by a component's internal state, its class names, or test-specific ids as a first choice — you query by what a user would actually perceive, primarily accessible roles, labels, and visible text. Clicking a button in a test means finding it the way a screen reader or a sighted user would find it, by its role and its accessible name, not by reaching into the rendered tree for a specific implementation detail.

This has a very deliberate side effect — writing tests this way tends to surface accessibility problems for free, because if a test can't find your button by its role, there's a real chance a screen reader user couldn't either.

It also means these tests are far less brittle to refactoring. If you change a component's internal state shape, its prop names, or even swap out how it's implemented entirely, a test written against user-visible behavior keeps passing, as long as the actual behavior the user experiences hasn't changed — which is exactly the kind of test that survives a refactor instead of blocking it.
bonus: The library's own query priority guide, ranking getByRole above things like getByTestId, is worth following closely — reaching for a test id is explicitly framed as a last resort, not a default first choice.

question: Question 44. How do you approach testing a custom hook, and when do you test it in isolation versus through a consuming component?
answer:
For a hook with genuinely independent logic — calculations, state transitions, something like a useDebouncedValue or a useForm hook — I test it in isolation using renderHook from React Testing Library. That utility renders the hook inside a minimal, invisible test component behind the scenes, and gives you back the hook's current return value along with a way to trigger re-renders and updates, so you can assert on the hook's behavior directly without needing any actual UI markup at all.

Testing in isolation is worth it specifically when the hook's logic is complex enough to deserve its own focused tests — several branches, edge cases around timing, or state transitions that would be tedious to trigger indirectly through a full component's UI.

For simpler hooks, or ones that are tightly coupled to how a specific component uses them, I often skip isolated testing and just test the component that uses the hook instead, through the same user-facing interactions I'd use for any other component test. If a hook only has one real consumer and its logic isn't especially complex, testing it in isolation can end up duplicating almost the same assertions the component test already covers, for very little extra confidence.

The deciding factor is really reusability and complexity — a hook meant to be reused across several components, or one with genuinely tricky internal logic, earns its own isolated tests. A hook that's really just an extracted implementation detail of one component usually doesn't need to be tested separately from that component.
bonus: renderHook also lets you pass different initial props and re-render with new ones, which is useful for testing exactly the dependency-array-driven behavior of hooks like a custom effect wrapper, without needing a full component around it.

question: Question 45. How do you mock network requests in component tests, and why do many teams prefer MSW over just mocking fetch directly?
answer:
Mocking fetch or an http client directly means replacing the actual function your code calls with a fake version that returns canned data. It works, but it ties your test to the exact way your component happens to make the request — which library it uses, how many times it's called, in what order — details that have nothing to do with the actual behavior you're trying to verify.

Mock Service Worker, MSW, takes a different approach — it intercepts requests at the network level, either through a real service worker in the browser or through request interception in Node for tests, and responds to them as if a real server had. Your component code makes an actual, unmodified fetch or axios call exactly as it would in production, and MSW answers it based on request handlers you define ahead of time, matching by URL and method.

This has a real practical benefit for tests — because your component isn't aware it's being mocked at all, you can refactor how a component fetches data, switch libraries, or add caching, and the test keeps working as long as the actual request being made still matches a handler. You're testing what the user experiences given a certain server response, not the specific mechanics of how the request was made.

It also lets you simulate realistic failure scenarios easily — a slow response, a server error, a malformed payload — by just defining a handler that responds that way, which is exactly the kind of edge case that's important to test but awkward to trigger against a real backend.
bonus: The same MSW handlers you write for tests can often be reused to run the whole app against a mocked backend during local development, which is a nice side benefit when a real backend isn't ready yet or is inconvenient to run locally.

question: Question 46. What changed with React Router's data APIs, loaders and actions, and how does that shift where data fetching logic lives?
answer:
Before the data APIs, data fetching in a routed app almost always lived inside the component itself, usually in a useEffect that fired after the route's component had already mounted — meaning there's an inherent delay between navigating to a route and that route actually starting to fetch its data.

Loaders move that fetching earlier and outside the component. You define a loader function for a route, and React Router calls it as part of the navigation itself, before rendering the destination route's component, and makes the result available to the component through a hook rather than fetching after the fact. Practically, this means the request for a route's data starts the moment navigation begins, not after the new component has already rendered — which removes a whole category of the render-then-fetch delay and the waterfalls that come with it.

Actions are the equivalent idea for mutations — form submissions and other data-changing operations get routed through an action function tied to the route, instead of a component managing submission state and calling an API directly in an event handler. React Router handles the surrounding lifecycle, like pending states during the submission and re-running the relevant loader afterward to refresh data.

The overall shift is treating data requirements as a property of a route itself, declared alongside the route definition, rather than as something implicit and scattered across whichever components happen to render for that route.
bonus: This same idea — data requirements declared at the routing layer instead of fetched inside components after they mount — is also the underlying philosophy behind frameworks like Remix, which React Router's data APIs share a lot of design lineage with.

question: Question 47. How do form libraries like React Hook Form differ from just managing form state with useState, and what validation patterns do you use with them?
answer:
Managing a form with individual useState calls per field works fine for something tiny, but it means every keystroke in any field re-renders the entire form component, since all the state lives at that top level. It also means validation logic, error state, and submission handling all have to be hand-rolled and kept consistent across every field yourself.

React Hook Form takes a different approach — it keeps most inputs uncontrolled internally, using refs rather than state, and only triggers a re-render for the specific fields that actually need to reflect a change, like a field currently showing a validation error. This avoids the whole-form re-render problem that a naive controlled approach has, especially noticeable in forms with many fields.

For validation, the common pattern is pairing the form library with a schema validation library like Zod or Yup, rather than writing validation rules as scattered inline functions per field.
