You define the shape and rules for the entire form's data once, as a schema, and the form library runs that schema against the current values, mapping any failures back to the specific fields they belong to. This has the added benefit that the same schema can often validate the payload again on the server, since it's just a plain description of what valid data looks like, not tied to any particular UI.

The validation timing itself is also a real design decision — validating on every keystroke feels responsive but can be noisy and show errors before someone's even finished typing, while validating only on submit can let someone fill out an entire long form before finding out early fields were wrong. A common middle ground is validating on blur, once someone leaves a field, and then switching to on-change validation for that specific field once it's already shown an error.
bonus: Deriving types directly from a Zod schema, rather than writing a separate TypeScript interface by hand for the same form, keeps the two from ever drifting out of sync as the form's fields change over time.

question: Question 48. What accessibility considerations come up specifically when designing a custom interactive component, like a modal or a combobox?
answer:
The first thing I think about is focus management. When a modal opens, focus needs to move into it, usually to the first focusable element or the modal itself, and it needs to be trapped inside the modal while it's open, so tabbing doesn't silently escape to elements behind it that a sighted user can't currently see are still there. When the modal closes, focus needs to return to whatever element originally opened it, so a keyboard user isn't left with focus on an element that no longer exists or makes no sense in context.

Keyboard interaction has to match what users actually expect from that kind of element, not just support keyboard use in some minimal way. A combobox needs arrow keys to move between options, enter to select, and escape to close, matching the expected pattern from native select elements and other comboboxes users have already learned, rather than inventing new keyboard behavior for the same familiar concept.

Aria attributes need to actually communicate the component's real state, not just be present. A custom dropdown needs an expanded state attribute reflecting whether it's actually open, and the currently focused or selected option needs to be communicated to screen reader users through an active descendant style attribute, since a screen reader has no other way to know which option is currently highlighted if focus itself doesn't visually move between them the way it would with plain links or buttons.

Because getting all of this fully right is genuinely hard, I lean toward headless, accessibility-focused libraries like React Aria or Radix for complex interactive patterns like comboboxes and menus, rather than building the full keyboard and aria behavior from scratch for every custom component.
bonus: Testing with an actual screen reader, even briefly, catches problems that visually inspecting the DOM or reading the aria attributes never will — a component can look correct in the accessibility tree and still be confusing or broken when you actually listen to how it's announced.

question: Question 49. As a component author, what do you need to be aware of regarding server rendering and hydration, even if you're not building the framework layer yourself?
answer:
When a component is server-rendered, it runs once on the server to produce HTML, and then runs again on the client during hydration, where React attaches event handlers and reconciles the client-rendered result against the server-rendered markup that's already sitting in the page. The core thing to be aware of is that these two renders need to produce matching output, or React will detect a mismatch and have to throw away and regenerate the affected part of the DOM on the client, which is both wasted work and, in some cases, a visible flash of content changing right after the page loads.

The most common source of mismatches is anything that depends on browser-only information during the initial render — reading the window's size, checking local storage, or using a random value or the current date directly in what gets rendered. The server doesn't have access to the same environment, so it renders something different than what the client will render for the same component on its first pass.

The fix for anything browser-only is to not read it during the render that has to match — instead, read it inside a useEffect, which only ever runs on the client after hydration has already completed, and update state from there if the component needs to react to it. This means the very first client render intentionally matches the server's output, and the browser-dependent behavior kicks in a moment later, after hydration, rather than trying to be correct from the very first render.

I keep this in mind even when I'm not personally setting up the server-rendering infrastructure, because it affects how any individual component should be written to be safe to render in an SSR context at all, regardless of which framework's server rendering is doing the actual heavy lifting.
bonus: A quick smell test for hydration risk is asking whether a component would render the same output twice in a row, in the same environment, with no side effects — if the answer depends on something that's different between a server process and a browser tab, that's a mismatch waiting to happen.

question: Question 50. When does it make sense to use TypeScript generics on a component's props, versus just writing a plain, specific prop type?
answer:
Generics earn their place when a component's behavior needs to stay consistent across different data shapes, but the shape of that data still needs to be fully type-checked for whoever's using the component. A generic List component that takes an array of items and a function to render each one is a good example — the component doesn't care what type the items are, but the render function passed in should still get properly typed items, not a loosely typed value, so a typo in a property name is still caught.

Without a generic, you'd either write that component against one specific item type, which makes it not actually reusable, or you'd type the items as something unknown, which technically works but throws away all the type safety a caller would actually want when working with their specific data.

The trade-off is complexity, both for whoever's reading the component's implementation and for the error messages TypeScript produces when something's used incorrectly. A component with two or three generic type parameters, especially with constraints between them, can become genuinely difficult to reason about, and a misuse of it can produce a wall of generic-related type errors that are hard to trace back to the actual mistake.

My rule of thumb is to reach for a generic when a component is intentionally designed to be data-shape agnostic and reused across genuinely different types in the same codebase — a generic Select, a generic Table, a generic List. For a component that's really only ever used with one specific shape of data in practice, even if it's theoretically reusable, a plain, specific prop type is easier to read, easier to get useful error messages from, and doesn't cost you anything you'd actually use.
bonus: Default type parameters can soften the complexity cost quite a bit — giving a generic component a sensible default type means most call sites don't need to think about the generic at all, and it only becomes visible to callers who actually need to override it.
